entry.title: Fully Autonomous Finance? Is that our Goal? (A Case for Human-in-the-Middle Scenarios)
entry.content:
In the rapidly evolving world of finance, AI is transforming how businesses operate. From automating routine tasks like transaction matching to summarizing and analyzing complex data sets including structured and non-structured information, AI offers remarkable efficiencies.

However, the human element remains crucial in the AI process, ensuring that technology serves its intended purpose effectively and ethically, while meeting specific compliance requirements.

Firstly, human oversight is essential for maintaining ethical standards. AI systems can process vast amounts of data, but they lack the ability to understand the nuances of ethical decision-making. Humans provide the moral compass, ensuring that AI applications align with societal values and regulatory requirements. This oversight helps prevent biases and ensures fairness.

SAP’s approach to Business AI addresses the requirements of ethical responsibility in AI – as well as relevance and reliability in AI. SAP has an AI ethics steering committee and an AI ethics advisory panel with industry leaders to ensure the ethical use of AI in the business context. These teams work with our customers for a more ethical use of AI that aligns with our core values.

Secondly, human expertise adds a layer of critical thinking that AI cannot replicate. While AI excels at identifying patterns and trends, it doesn't possess the intuition and judgment that come from years of experience. Financial professionals can interpret AI-generated insights, considering broader economic contexts and existing relationships with business partners and networks. This leads to more informed decisionmaking considering factors that machines alone might miss. It’s then the combination of the human and AI that delivers more value that leads to better decisionmaking.

Example:  Finance experts provide detailed commentary on financial results. AI can generate a summary that combines the commentary with financial analysis, making a well-explained, comprehensive view of business performance available to business managers that may not be financial experts.

In the world of finance, there could be a legal burden of proof when decisions lead to results that destroy shareholder value. For example, in Germany, this applies to both the management boards of publicly traded stock corporations (AG) as well as limited liability companies (GmbH), regardless of their size. In these cases, company officers might be required to show the data they used as a basis for their decisions[1]. This often includes demonstrating how the risks and opportunities were weighed, and that the decisions that were made can be reasonably considered to be “in the best interest of the organization”. If AI is making decisions autonomously, it might be impossible to re-create the data foundation of the decision. This could leave company officers liable to legal consequences.

Moreover, humans play a vital role in balancing risk and rewards, based on professional experience. AI systems, though powerful, are not infallible. They can make errors or be vulnerable to cyber threats. Human involvement ensures continuous monitoring and quick intervention when anomalies occur, safeguarding financial systems from potential disruptions.

AI at SAP: Human-in-the-Middle

At SAP, as AI is embedded into finance business processes, the processes are designed to keep experts involved. In the early days of AI, machine learning provided recommendations for expert intervention based on previous experience – for example suggesting a write-off of a particilar goods-and-invoice-receipt reconciliation issue. The system proposed the “(most-likely) next step” option for moving the process forward. The accountant would then restart the process with a single click.

With the evolution of generative AI, SAP Business AI can propose the mapping of one chart of accounts to a standard chart, suggest configuration settings for confirmation, and even explain the accounting details behind the evolution of asset values over times.

The current focus of many teams currently are AI agents. AI agents act as co-workers for accountants, assisting in real time. The accountant has the experience of having their own dedicated, super-personalized assistant that knows all your data, speeds up tasks, and enhances decision-making.

AI agents handle the most simple processing tasks and summarize decision options for the accountant. In these cases, the accountant is making the key decisions, and the follow-on actions are triggered via workflows that are performed by a combination of real and virtual agents.

In these examples you can see that AI supports and assists the accountant for more effective outcomes. While AI prepares analyses for decisionmaking, it’s the human that makes the decision.

Example: AI can use your company’s specific accounting policy and your sales forecast data to derive accrual postings related to sales commissions. However, rather than post these accruals directly, the system generates a posting proposal which is confirmed by the local accountant and then approved by the accounting manager. The journal posting is triggered only after this principle of dual control is followed. This reduces compliance risk for the CFO who remains personally responsible for the completeness and accuracy of corporate financial reports in many jurisdications. Read this blog for more details.

In conclusion, while AI revolutionizes finance, the human touch remains indispensable. By integrating human insight and control, businesses can harness AI's full potential, ensuring a future where technology and accountants work hand in hand. Accountants are not going to be replaced by AI. Accountants who use AI effectively will replace accountants who don’t.

 

Learn more about SAP Business AI in Finance here: https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/finance.html

 

(And thanks to my colleague Daniel Welzbacher for his input!)

[1] Business Judgement Rule (§93(1)(2) Germany’s Stock Corporation Act (AktG)).

entry.title: Do you know which services in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition are performed by SAP?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Administering SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - Interaction with SAP learning journey.

Overview
After completing this learning journey, the learners will be able to identify which services in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition are performed by SAP, and which services can only be performed by the customer. Additionally, they will understand the use cases for SAP Service requests including their process flow and how to create Service Requests to interact with SAP, what input SAP requires to accomplish an ordered task, and what output to expect.

Learning objectives
This course will prepare to:

  • Explain the meaning of the Roles and Responsibilities document
  • Classify services in the context of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • Explain the use of SAP Cloud Application Services
  • Illustrate the Service Process Flow using examples.

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area. 

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you. 

I appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Introduction to SAP S/4HANA Advanced Intercompany Sales
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA Advanced Intercompany Sales is a robust solution designed to streamline and optimize the sales processes between affiliated companies within a corporate group. This functionality is particularly useful for organizations where a local sales office sells products to external customers, but the goods are delivered directly from a producing company or a central warehouse. The process ensures seamless integration between sales, procurement, delivery, and billing operations, enabling efficient intercompany transactions.

Business Process Overview

 

The Advanced Intercompany Sales process involves the following key steps:

  1. Sales Order Creation: A standard sales order is created by the selling company for an external customer. The system automatically identifies if the delivering plant belongs to a different company code, making the order relevant for intercompany processing.
  2. Intercompany Purchase Order: Upon saving the sales order, the system generates an intercompany purchase order in the selling company. This purchase order is directed to the delivering company, which is responsible for supplying the goods.
  3. Intercompany Sales Order: The delivering company receives the purchase order and creates an intercompany sales order. This order is used to manage the internal transaction between the delivering company and the selling company.
  4. Delivery and Goods Movement: The delivering company processes the outbound delivery and ships the goods directly to the external customer. The system manages the goods movements, including the transfer of goods into and out of stock in transit, ensuring accurate inventory tracking.
  5. Billing: Two types of invoices are generated:
    • Customer Invoice: The selling company bills the external customer for the goods delivered.
    • Intercompany Customer Invoice: The delivering company bills the selling company for the intercompany transaction.
  6. Invoice Verification: The intercompany supplier invoice is verified against the intercompany customer invoice to ensure accuracy and compliance.

Key Features

  • Real-Time Integration: The process is fully integrated into the SAP S/4HANA value chain monitoring framework, allowing real-time tracking and monitoring of each step.
  • Valuated Stock in Transit: The system uses valuated stock in transit to manage the transfer of goods between affiliated companies, ensuring accurate financial reporting and inventory management.
  • Automated Workflows: Many steps, such as the creation of intercompany purchase orders and sales orders, are automated, reducing manual effort and minimizing errors.

Setup Steps

To enable Advanced Intercompany Sales in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502, the following setup steps are required:

  1. Enable the Process: Activate the Advanced Intercompany Sales process (scope item 5D2) through expert configuration. This involves enabling specific pre-delivered configurations.
  2. Assign Purchasing Organization and Group: Assign a purchasing organization and purchasing group to the sales organization of the intercompany sales order.
  3. Configure Pricing: Set up pricing independently for the delivering and selling companies. This includes defining pricing procedures and condition types for internal and external customers.
  4. Define Transfer of Control Dates: Configure the internal and external transfer of control dates to manage the timing of goods movements and revenue recognition.
  5. Monitor Value Chains: Use the Monitor Value Chains app to orchestrate and monitor each step of the intercompany sales process.

Analyzing the Process

To analyze and resolve issues in the Advanced Intercompany Sales process, you can use the following tools:

  • Sales Order Fulfillment - Analyze and Resolve Issues App: This app helps identify and resolve issues that may impede the fulfillment of sales orders. It provides insights into critical stages of the process and facilitates collaboration with relevant contacts.
  • Monitor Value Chains App: This app offers a comprehensive view of the entire intercompany sales process, allowing you to track the status of sales orders, deliveries, and invoices.

Conclusion

SAP S/4HANA Advanced Intercompany Sales provides a comprehensive solution for managing complex intercompany sales processes. By automating key steps and integrating them into a unified framework, it enhances efficiency, accuracy, and visibility across the supply chain. Proper setup and configuration, along with the use of analytical tools, ensure smooth operation and timely resolution of any issues that may arise.

entry.title: Service with Advanced Execution: Process Flow 6 – Preventive or Recurring Service Processing
entry.content:
Service with Advanced Execution is a new service business process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. This is based on the integration of Service with Maintenance Management.

The concept of Execution Status in Service with Advanced Execution is introduced in the following blog:

Execution Status in Service with Advanced Execution

Within Service with Advanced Execution, there are various process flows depending on the business scenario. These process flows are distinguished based on two aspects:

  • How the process starts, and which is the first transaction.
  • What is the trigger for the creation of the maintenance order and eventually the start of planning.

Process Flow 6:

This blog showcases the process flow wherein the initial transaction is the service contract and the maintenance plan. On scheduling the maintenance plan, both the documents are created simultaneously i.e. service order with execution order item and billable maintenance order along with the linkage between the two.

Maintenance plans enable the scheduling of recurring services at predefined intervals. These services can be part of routine maintenance on a periodic basis or triggered based on usage or performance.

For e.g. A car dealership offers preventive maintenance services for customers who have purchased vehicles from them. As part of the service agreement, the dealership provides regular doorstep maintenance every 6 months, to ensure optimal vehicle performance and longevity. A time-based maintenance plan can be set up for the car. After every 6 months, the maintenance plan triggers the creation of two orders:

  • A Service Order (the commercial object).
  • A Maintenance Order (the execution object with task list).

The service order and the maintenance order are linked with each other through document flow.

In addition to the routine maintenance, the dealership also recommends a battery replacement every 2 years. This service can be planned with a strategy-based maintenance plan.

Creation of Service Contract

A service contract can be created using ‘Manage Service Contracts’ App.
The following details could be filled while creating a service contract:

  • Sold-to-Party
  • Organizational data such as Sales Organization
  • Price information such as price agreement
  • Parties involved
  • Item details such as billing plan, price information, object list, product list etc.

Creation of Maintenance Plan

A maintenance plan can be created using ‘Add Single Plan’, ‘Add Strategy Controlled Plan’ or ‘Add Multiple Counter Plan’ Apps to create time-based or performance-based single cycle plans, strategy plans or multiple counter maintenance plans respectively.

The following details could be filled while creating a maintenance plan:

  1. Select the maintenance plan category.
  2. On the Item tab, enter a new item by specifying item description and technical object. In the planning data section enter service contract, service contract item, and order type. The service contract is mandatory.
  3. Provide a name for the maintenance plan and enter the cycle details. Optionally, enter a task list.
  4. Enter the scheduling period under maintenance plan scheduling parameters tab to specify the number of days, months, or years over which scheduling will take place.
  5. The ‘Do Not Rel. Immediately’ checkbox can be marked, if the maintenance order must not be released immediately once created.

Scheduling of Maintenance Plan

Once a maintenance plan is created, the next step is to schedule it. In the ‘Schedule Maintenance Plan' App, do the following:

  1. Enter the plan number created.
  2. When Start is clicked, the scheduling list gets populated with all scheduled calls and the dates for which they have been scheduled.
  3. Once Save is clicked, depending on the call dates, the service order and its corresponding maintenance order is generated as call objects.

The service product in the service order is copied from the service contract as the execution order item. The item category for this service product is determined from the ‘Copy Item Categories - Item Category Determination’ customizing.

Similarly, the Sold-to-Party in the service order is also taken over from the service contract.

While creating the service order and the maintenance order, the order type mapping configurations (SPRO -> Service -> Transactions -> Settings for Service Transactions -> Integration -> Plant Maintenance Integration-> Map Order Types) are not relevant. The service transaction type and plant maintenance order type are user inputs in the maintenance plan.

The operations and components in the maintenance order are derived from the task list entered in the maintenance plan.

Status lifecycle in Service with Advanced Execution

                                                      Current state of an execution order item

The state diagram illustrates the status lifecycle of one execution order item along with its corresponding maintenance order in this process flow. Each block below represents a state displaying 3 different status values. The lifecycle and execution status of an execution order item along with the status of the corresponding maintenance order.

 

Release of Execution Order Item

Once the service order and execution order item details are finalized, the lifecycle status can be set to ‘Released’. The execution status on the execution order item is ‘Execution Planning’ and the maintenance order status is 'CRTD' (Created).

Note: The execution order item can be rejected only when the execution status is ‘Execution Planning’.

The document flow between the service order and maintenance order can be viewed in Transaction History assignment block. The maintenance plan and maintenance item document flow can also be viewed here.

The details of the maintenance order connected to the execution order item can be viewed in the Maintenance Order assignment block in the item.

Release of Maintenance Order

Once the maintenance order is created from the service order, the maintenance order is in planning phase. After the planning is done, the maintenance order is set to ‘Released’ by the Maintenance Planner. Then the execution of service can be started. The maintenance order status is changed from ‘CRTD’ to ‘REL’ (Released). With this status transition, the execution status on the execution order item is set to ‘In Execution’.

Completion of Execution Order Item

After the execution of service is completed by the Maintenance Technician, the maintenance order status is set to ‘TECO’ (Technically Completed).  With this status transition, the lifecycle status of execution order item is set to ‘Completed’. The execution status on the execution order item is set to ‘Execution Done’.

Once the service order is set to ‘Completed’, the completion date is updated for the call in maintenance plan scheduling list.

Summary

The distinct feature in this process flow is the creation service order and maintenance order from maintenance plan. As we proceed with the execution of maintenance order, the lifecycle and execution status of execution order item in service order are in continuous alignment with the status of maintenance order at every step.

 

Further references

To know more about the execution order item and its attributes, please refer following blog:

Deep Dive: Execution Order Item and Billable Maintenance Order

entry.title: Back Order Processing - Advanced Available-to-Promise (aATP in S/4HANA) version 2023 Part II
entry.content:
Advanced Available to Promise (aATP) 2023 – Part 2 

SAP S/4HANA Advanced Available-to-Promise (aATP) is a critical component of the S/4HANA suite that enhances the standard ATP (Available-to-Promise) functionality with more advanced and flexible features. It is designed to meet complex order fulfilment processes and ensure higher accuracy and efficiency in inventory allocation and customer order management.

Backorder Processing (BOP)

What is BOP in S/4HANA

In simple word BOP is nothing but rescheduling of all existing orders with some specific rules with some predefined parameters. BOP was also available in ECC system with less parameters for re-scheduling. In S/4HANA now we have many parameters by which we can reschedule the orders and can allocate, distribute and redistribute the quantities between the sales orders in more efficient way.

We have a scenario where we have committed to fulfilling multiple sales orders based on the quantities and inbound delivery dates outlined in a purchase order. However, the vendor has now informed you of a production delay, and only a partial delivery will be possible.

As a result, you need to update the confirmed delivery dates and quantities for both your sales and inventory orders to reflect these changes. To do all this process in mass BOP will help business in accurate way.

Back-order processing in ECC Vs in S/4HANA

Classical rescheduling selects all requirements for a given material/plant combination.

However, BOP (Backorder Processing) allows for more targeted selection of requirements using various criteria. Additionally, the options for sorting these requirements have been enhanced. Selection and sorting criteria are defined within a "segment." One or more segments are linked to a BOP variant and executed together. The segments linked to a variant can employ one of five different confirmation strategies, which determine the extent to which the requirements should be confirmed.

When multiple segments with different strategies are part of a variant, the confirmation quantities will be higher (and closer to the requested date) based on the priority of the strategy used.

Within aATP, SAP S/4HANA has introduced a new confirmation strategy that categorizes your customers into five different groups:

  • Win: When demand exceeds supply, these customers receive top priority for confirmation and fulfilment.
  • Gain: These customers retain their original confirmations when demand exceeds supply, and additional orders will be confirmed if stock remains after all Win group orders are fulfilled.
  • Redistribute: These customers may lose some of their original commitments to the Win or Gain groups when demand exceeds supply. However, if there is remaining stock after these groups are confirmed, they will be prioritized next.
  • Fill: These customers function similarly to the Redistribute group but won’t receive any additional confirmations.
  • Lose: When demand exceeds supply, these customers will lose their confirmations to higher-priority groups.

BOP set up with Fiori apps -

Let us see how we can setup BOP for sales process in SAP using Fiori apps. Below are the apps which we can use to activate BOP in S/4HANA

  1. Configure BOP segment

This feature enables you to establish sorting guidelines for prioritizing various needs, like sales orders and stock transfers, during a Bill of Process (BOP) execution. You have the flexibility to establish criteria based on factors such as customer importance, delivery deadlines, order categories, Available-to-Promise (ATP) classifications, and custom fields, guaranteeing that the most critical demands are handled at the forefront. This sorting takes place within each individual segment of the BOP.

In the 'Configure BOP Segment' app, you need to maintain the sort attribute and sort sequence. The sort attribute refers to standard parameters such as delivery priority, order type, or document category and so on.

Create segments -

Created DF_BOP_WIN with Selection criteria 1 (High) and Sort attribute with delivery priority and sales document type.

  1. Configure Custom BOP Sorting –

Set Up the BOP Segment Application

This application assists in forming BOP segments by combining like requirements - such as sales orders, stock transfer orders (STOs), and reservations - considering factors like material, plant location, customer, order type, and ATP categories. These segments then allow for focused backorder processing using various BOP strategies such as Win, Gain, Improve, Redistribute, Fill, and Lose. You can also link multiple segments to the same strategy in a specific order.

📌For instance, you might establish one segment for high-priority export orders and another for regular domestic orders, making sure each is handled with the appropriate strategy.

The 'Configure Custom BOP Sorting' app is used to set priorities for specific values. For example, if the sort attribute is set to order type, you must define the priority sequence, such as assigning priority 1 to OR, followed by ZOR, and then YOR.

In the 'Configure BOP Segment' app, you must assign the Sort Sequence you created in the 'Configure Custom BOP Sorting' app.

Users can specify the selection criteria and the sorting sequence for the requirement documents. To the right the specific sorting definition is shown.

 

  1. Configure BOP Variant

As per me BOP variant like a game plan. You get to choose the different BOP strategies you want to use and then match them up with the right BOP segments. Each strategy can have more than one segment linked to it.

When you assign several BOP segments to a single strategy, they will run in the order you set them up. In this part, you will take those BOP segments you set up earlier (in Step 1, where you configured them) and connect them to any of the available strategies, like Win, Gain, Improve, Redistribute, Fill, or even Lose.

The BOP variant acts like a filter, deciding which requirements get included in the process and how they are handled. These set variants are what you will use later on to plan your BOP run. Once everything is set up, the system lets you test out the variant in real-time, and you can even make a copy of it to use with a different strategy.

  1. Schedule BOP Run

Once the BOP variant is created, users can then indicate which variant they'd like to have scheduled. The job can be set to run right away or at a specific time in the future, and users can also set how often it should run. Users have the choice between running a simulation or an actual execution that will update orders.

Users can also turn on the log feature, which creates logs that are then available for viewing within the BOP monitoring application.

 

  1. Monitor BOP Run

Once Job is finised we can Monitor BOP run.

The executed jobs including simulations, can be seen here with details of:

  • Variant name, username, job details and processing status
  • Overall confirmations and details of old confirmation quantity vs. the new confirmation quantity
  • Overall supply assignment quantity and details of old vs. new assignment quantities

📌Let us take live example to check the BOP run result

I created two sales orders, 27 and 28, with quantities of 100 and 50, respectively. Both orders have a customer priority of 2 (Medium).

Currently, there is no stock available, so the quantities remain unconfirmed. To expedite the process, I uploaded 150 units. After running the BOP, the full quantity for both sales orders was successfully confirmed.

After the job is completed, we can track sales order confirmation changes using the 'Monitor BOP Run' app. The system displays both the previous and updated allocations for sales orders, as shown in the example below.

Sales orders 27 and 28 now have fully confirmed quantities, with a delivery priority of 2 (Medium).

Now, let's create a sales order for a different customer with a delivery priority of 1 (High). Upon creation, the system initially confirms zero quantity. The expected behaviour is that the system should redistribute stock from the first two sales orders and allocate it to the new sales order 29 after the BOP run, in line with our WIN strategy.

As shown in the screenshot above, after the BOP run, the system removed the confirmation of 100 units from sales order 27 and 50 units from sales order 28. These released quantities were then allocated to sales order 29, even though it was created later in the system.

Fallback Functionality in SAP Backorder Processing (BOP)

In SAP Advanced ATP (aATP) Backorder Processing (BOP), the fallback functionality ensures that requirements (such as sales orders or stock transfer orders) still receive an allocation if they do not meet the criteria of their initially assigned BOP strategy (Win, Gain, Improve, Redistribute, Fill, Lose).

Example Scenario

- Sales Order A is assigned to the WIN strategy, but due to stock shortages, it cannot be fully confirmed.

- Instead of remaining unconfirmed, the system reassigns it to a fallback strategy like GAIN or REDISTRIBUTE, allowing it to receive at least a partial allocation.

- This helps maintain a balanced and efficient stock allocation process.
